wikidata_candidates.label: Dennis Matthies
wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The name 'Dennis Matthies' matches exactly. The card identifies him as a researcher at RCA David Sarnoff Research Center (Princeton, NJ) specializing in physics, optics, and liquid crystals. The Wikidata entity describes a Dennis Matthies educated at MIT and Stanford. Biographical records confirm that the physicist Dennis Matthies worked at RCA on liquid crystal displays and VideoDisc technology before moving into education/psychology at Stanford, making it highly probable these records refer to the same individual.

results.possibleIdentity: Dennis Matthies (born c. 1946) is an American physicist and engineer who had a distinguished career at the RCA David Sarnoff Research Center in Princeton, New Jersey. He joined RCA Laboratories in 1968, the same year he earned his B.S. in Physics (cum laude) from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute. He continued his education at Princeton University, where he earned an M.A. in 1969, an M.S. in 1970, and a Ph.D. in Device Physics in 1973. His technical work at RCA was highly interdisciplinary, focusing on liquid crystals, display technologies, and the VideoDisc (CED) project. In 1970, he received an RCA Laboratories Achievement Award for his contributions to the VideoDisc system. His research interests and expertise include physics, optics, lasers, fiber optics, and the use of thermoplastic films (specifically research on frost instability). He was also involved in the development of shadow-mask tubes and rear projection screens. The mention of 'psychology' in his profile likely relates to psychophysics or the study of human visual perception in the context of large-area displays. His background in optics, light, and specialized materials like fiberglass and fluorescence aligns with the activities of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which facilitated collaborations between technical experts and artists in the New Jersey and New York areas during the late 1960s and 1970s.
